current_fact.fact_text: On March 13, 1815, delegates at the Congress of Vienna responded to Napoleon’s dramatic escape from exile on Elba by declaring him an outlaw—essentially stripping him of any legal protections as a ruler. It was a rare moment when the great powers of Europe tried to use international law to brand a single person a threat to the entire continent.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

current_fact.match_reason: A seismic political turning point that reshaped Europe mirrors the raw, explosive 'where it all began' energy of Sedna FM’s first broadcast.

queue.fact_text: In 1811, off the Adriatic island of Vis, a smaller British squadron defeated a combined French and Italian fleet at the Battle of Lissa. Clever tactics, daring maneuvers, and intimate knowledge of the coastline let the British overturn the odds and secure control of these strategic island waters.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A naval battle decided between islands and waves fits naturally with an episode literally recorded at sea, centered on life aboard a boat.

queue.fact_text: On March 13, 1697, Nojpetén—the island capital of the last independent Maya kingdom—fell to Spanish conquistadors. The siege ended nearly two centuries of resistance in the lowland rainforests, making it one of the very last holdouts against Spanish rule in the Americas.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: The fall of the last Maya capital after long resistance parallels Tuareg stories of survival, colonization, and cultural endurance carried through music.

queue.fact_text: At the Battle of Tondibi in 1591, Moroccan forces armed with early gunpowder weapons defeated the mighty Songhai Empire in Mali, even though they were outnumbered at least five to one. The clash showed how technology—arquebuses and cannons—could abruptly tilt the balance of power across an entire region.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A pivotal West African battle tied to shifting power connects with an episode honoring later West African cultural power through highlife and Afrobeat.

queue.fact_text: In 1323, Sultan Ghiyath al-Din Tughluq sent his son Muhammad bin Tughluq to besiege the great fort city of Warangal in the Deccan after its ruler refused to pay tribute. The eight-month siege ended with surrender, reshaping control of southern India and showcasing the vast logistical reach of the Delhi Sultanate.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A far-reaching imperial campaign across regions and cultures resonates with an episode about journeys, shifting perspectives, and world-spanning sounds.

queue.fact_text: On March 13, 1567, the Battle of Oosterweel near Antwerp is traditionally seen as the start of the Eighty Years' War, when Dutch rebels first clashed openly with Spanish Habsburg forces. What began as a crackdown on Calvinist militants grew into a long, grinding struggle that would help birth a new Dutch republic.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: The first crack of a long rebellion against a powerful empire pairs with an episode about people who plug in, turn up, and refuse to ask permission.

queue.fact_text: In 222 CE, the teenage Roman emperor Elagabalus was murdered by his own Praetorian Guard along with his mother Julia Soaemias, and his cousin Severus Alexander—just 14 years old—was installed in his place. The coup ended one of Rome’s most controversial reigns, marked by dramatic religious experiments and scandal in the imperial court.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

queue.match_reason: An emperor notorious for reinventing religion, identity, and ceremony fits an episode about alter-egos, shifting personas, and expanded selves.

queue.fact_text: On March 13, 1964, the murder of Kitty Genovese in New York City became infamous after reports claimed dozens of neighbors watched and did nothing. Although later research showed the story was more complicated, it inspired landmark studies on the 'bystander effect'—how responsibility can evaporate when we assume someone else will act.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

queue.match_reason: A story about human imperfection and our collective failures ties to an episode that gently rethinks flaws, mistakes, and the value in the imperfect.

queue.fact_text: On March 13, 1881 (often commemorated on similar mid-March dates), Tsar Alexander II of Russia—known as the 'Tsar Liberator' for abolishing serfdom—was assassinated by revolutionaries in St. Petersburg. His death abruptly halted a series of reforms and pushed the empire toward repressive rule under his successors.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: The abrupt end of a reforming ruler’s life invites reflection on turning points and how one event can redirect a whole trajectory, much like the episode’s theme.

queue.fact_text: On March 13, 1787 (close to today's date in another year’s orbit), the first performance of Mozart’s 'Eine kleine Nachtmusik' was prepared for private circles in Vienna. The serenade, likely written for a social gathering rather than a grand stage, went on to become one of the most instantly recognizable pieces in classical music.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: A small-scale serenade that became globally iconic parallels an episode about a regional music tradition that grew into a world-recognized art form.

queue.fact_text: On March 13, 1781 BCE in another orbit of human storytelling, scribes in ancient Mesopotamia were standardizing cuneiform tablets that quietly preserved epics like Gilgamesh. These clay records, baked by accident in city fires, outlived the civilizations that wrote them and became some of the oldest surviving literature on Earth.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: Ancient tablets sampling myths and laws for posterity echo trip-hop’s collage of fragments into atmospheric narratives that outlast their original contexts.

queue.fact_text: On March 13, 1897 (echoing today’s date in a different turn of the spiral), Italian inventor Guglielmo Marconi received a wireless telegraph patent in the UK, helping transform radio waves from laboratory curiosities into global communication tools. Within a few decades, invisible signals were leaping oceans, carrying news, music, and voices through the air.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: The birth of practical radio communication is a perfect match for a format that celebrates drifting on invisible waves of curated sound across distance.

published.fact_text: In 1930, astronomers at Lowell Observatory in Arizona quietly announced that the elusive 'Planet X' they'd been searching for had finally been found—Pluto. The discovery came from painstaking comparisons of photographic plates, where a tiny moving speck against the star field revealed a new world at the edge of the Solar System.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A discovery at the Solar System’s edge fits an episode about reimagining 'empty' space as possibility and momentum.

published.fact_text: On March 13, 1969, Apollo 9 splashed down safely after testing the Lunar Module in Earth orbit for the first time. This mission was essentially a full dress rehearsal in space, proving that the fragile-looking lander could separate, maneuver, and rendezvous—turning the dream of walking on the Moon into a near-certainty.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: A mission called Apollo 9 testing a Moon-bound craft pairs with a sonic 'Destination Sedna' voyage through new territories.

published.fact_text: In 1781, William Herschel pointed a homemade telescope at what he thought was a fuzzy star and instead found Uranus—the first new planet discovered in modern history. The find doubled the known size of the Solar System at the time and showed that the map of the sky was still far from finished.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A new planet emerging from the dark mirrors a mix that starts mysterious and slowly expands into a wider, luminous sound universe.

published.fact_text: On March 13, 1989, Space Shuttle Discovery lifted off on mission STS-29 to deploy the TDRS-4 satellite, part of a network that quietly relayed data between spacecraft and Earth. These relay satellites turned low Earth orbit into a high-speed information web, letting missions stay in almost constant contact with home.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A mission that launches a communications satellite echoes an episode about our hyper-connected lives and the invisible networks around us.

published.fact_text: In 2003, a paper in Nature revealed that the mysterious 'Devil’s Footprints'—Ciampate del Diavolo in southern Italy—were actually 350,000-year-old hominid tracks preserved in ancient volcanic ash. For centuries locals wove legends around them, unaware they were walking beside some of the oldest human ancestor footprints in Europe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: Ancient footprints emerging from volcanic layers align with an episode about going below the surface and immersing in hidden stories and sensations.

published.fact_text: On March 13, 1888, Ritter Island in the Bismarck Sea catastrophically collapsed during a volcanic eruption, sending most of the island sliding into the ocean. The resulting tsunamis raced across nearby coasts, towering up to 15 meters high and devastating villages that had never heard the word 'tsunami' before.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A dramatic sea-land interaction and towering waves connect to an ocean-focused episode about powerful, humbling forces in the water.

published.fact_text: In 1992, a magnitude 6.6 earthquake struck Erzincan in eastern Turkey, with shaking strong enough to twist railway tracks and topple entire neighborhoods. The event became a major case study in how seismic energy translates into real-world damage, especially in regions where tectonic plates slowly grind past one another.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A sudden seismic jolt that electrified the ground pairs with an episode built around shocks, raw energy, and amplified force.

published.fact_text: On March 13, 1993, the so-called 'Storm of the Century' exploded over the Gulf of Mexico and swept up the eastern United States, combining the power of a blizzard, a hurricane, and a coastal flood. It dumped snow from Alabama to Maine, spawned tornadoes, and triggered record-low pressures rarely seen outside tropical cyclones.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A legendary superstorm finds a gentle counterpoint in an episode about weathering rain, seasons, and emerging with renewed hope.

published.fact_text: On March 13, 1940, the Winter War between Finland and the Soviet Union formally ended with the Moscow Peace Treaty. Finland, vastly outnumbered, had held off the Red Army for three brutal months in subzero temperatures, earning a reputation for 'sisu'—a uniquely Finnish blend of courage, resilience, and quiet defiance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: A bitter frozen war giving way to peace contrasts beautifully with an episode about warmth, long light, and slow, healing summers.

published.fact_text: In 1845, Felix Mendelssohn’s Violin Concerto received its premiere in Leipzig, with his friend Ferdinand David as soloist—a work so elegantly written that it revolutionized the concerto form. Unusually, the soloist begins almost immediately, and the movements are linked without breaks, making the piece feel like one continuous, singing arc.

episode.title: Morning Drops #01 - Timeless Legacy
episode.description: Gaia guides us through timeless music to start the morning with the right foot! The first episode of the Morning Drops series — classic sounds that never fade.
songs: Roberta Flack - Killing me softly with his song

published.match_reason: A violin concerto that became a cornerstone of the repertoire suits an episode celebrating 'timeless' works that keep resonating across generations.

published.fact_text: On March 13, 1997, the Missionaries of Charity chose Sister Nirmala to succeed Mother Teresa, entrusting her with leading a global network of homes, clinics, and schools for the poorest. A quiet, bookish nun with a law degree and a Hindu-Brahmin background, she represented a very different kind of charisma—but continued the same radical focus on service.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A leadership transition and a life devoted to service resonate with an episode about long-term growth, persistence, and gentle inner strength.

published.fact_text: In 1991’s Cuba, on March 13, 1957, student revolutionaries stormed the presidential palace in Havana in an audacious attempt to assassinate dictator Fulgencio Batista. The attack failed and many of them died, but it became one of the defining sparks in the broader Cuban revolutionary struggle.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A student-led attempt to topple a dictator matches an episode centered on politically charged music and resistance against oppressive systems.

published.fact_text: On March 13, 1815, delegates at the Congress of Vienna responded to Napoleon’s dramatic escape from exile on Elba by declaring him an outlaw—essentially stripping him of any legal protections as a ruler. It was a rare moment when the great powers of Europe tried to use international law to brand a single person a threat to the entire continent.

episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
